speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three, March 24, 1996. My name is Susan Peirez speaking with Eli Stern.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were ordering us [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to-- to walk. As I say, the dogs are barking. The confusion is beyond belief. We see a line of trucks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before we realize it, we see all the old men and old women on the trucks. Children that weren't lucky enough to be held by a-- a older sibling or a parent, a lot of children were lost in the confusion, were just thrown onto the trucks like you throw a cord of wood. It was-- it wasn't picked up and put on the truck. It was just thrown into the truck. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If I may go back to the disembarking of the-- of the-- of the train, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when those wagons opened up and we're told not to take anything with us, because we're not going to need anything with us, we see a group of hungry-looking, thin people with striped prisoner clothes. They all had a number on their chest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they say to us in Yiddish, you fools, why did you come? Why didn't-- why did you let them bring you here? We didn't know what they were talking about. Some of us say to them, what is going to happen to us? He says, don't you see that chimney? Do you see those flames coming out? We still don't know what it meant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not allowed to talk to us openly, but they were-- they got that-- they talked to us when they weren't watched. Those were people that were in Auschwitz way before, for several years now. And it was their job to get us unloaded. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As we were off the trains now, and we're in the-- on the-- on the railroad siding, we see German officers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they're dividing the men, or the able-bodied men and women from the either old people or women that had babies in their arms. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Very, very shortly after we disembarked, my father was shown to go to one side of the-- where it turned out to be the people that were kept for-- for work. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- my mother is walking ahead of me with the youngest child in her arm. I'm walking behind her with one brother on each side holding on to them. I'm in the middle. As I'm walking, I find-- I feel like a cane, a thing that has a round handle with a cane that you hold, a cane on my neck. And I'm being pulled. I just dropped my-- the hands of my two brothers on each side of me. And he shows me to go to the-- to the other side. I didn't say goodbye to my mother. To this day, I-- I have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a guilt conscious of not even saying goodbye. I-- I was confused. I was scared. And the guy told me to move over. I just did. That's the last time I saw my mother and my siblings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, I am with a lot of people that are on the side, so-called, on the side that were going to live. But I knew my father was taken there. Now, I am on that side. But I can't find my father. I'm all of-- I wasn't even 14 at the time. I'm panicky. I'm looking for a familiar face. I'm looking for-- I'm calling my father by name. I knew if I called dad, there was a lot of guys that called dad. So I call him by name. I cannot find my father. I'm getting desperate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father used to have a-- a habit where he would like clear his throat. Back in the good old days, so to speak, at home, he would go like, [CLEARS THROAT]. And I hear that clearing of that throat. And that was music to my ears. I knew it was my father by the way he cleared his throat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I ran to that-- to that noise of-- of clearing of the throat. And I'm together with my father now. He wants to know why I am not with my mother. I said, this German officer told me to come on this side. So now, we are all together. My father was with me. There's a group of people that are-- the group consists of all the people from my town that made it up to that point, and people that we got to make acquaintances with in the ghetto. We're all a group of familiar faces. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We see the chimneys. We feel a smell. But we don't know-- we don't know what is happening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's still in-- it's still in the middle of the night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They're bringing us in to a big building. And they're telling us all to undress. And the only thing that we can take with us is our shoes and go to the next-- to another room in the building where they shave everybody's head. And we're being put in a room [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where when we walk into that room, we see the women that made it to that point. Their hair was shaven. They were facing one wall. We were facing another wall. And to look at these women without their hair, it looked like-- like mannequins that you look in a-- at a window. Some people saw their daughters. Some people saw their sisters. But we weren't able to get together or talk to them. We were in a huge, big room. They were facing one way, and we were facing another way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have clothes on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're all naked. The only thing that we have in our hands is our shoes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We're given orders to go into another room. We're walking out one door. The women are walking out the other door. I haven't seen a female from that point on till after we were liberated. Because in the camps, we were separated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we went on to the other room, with our hair, all naked with our shoes in our hands, they're issuing us, it looks like pajamas-- striped clothes. We got the striped clothes. There was no question as to size. You just were given clothes. So-- and we're all issued numbers. We were not tattooed, but we were issued numbers. My number was 55599. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We're all dressed in these striped prisoner clothes. We're all given a tin plate and a-- and a spoon. That was our whole belonging for the rest of the stay in the camps. That's all we-- that was our-- all that we had for the rest of the stay in the camp. They are marching us to a barrack. They called it a block. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're coming into the block. The beds over there is what you see in a-- in a warehouse, like big, wide shelves. I would say six or eight people were sleeping on one of those shelves. It was-- I don't remember if it was two or three stories high. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are in that. We are assigned this block. Everybody is ordered to go outside, line up. We had to line up five-- five abreast. We had to all line up. And we were standing there for a long time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Finally, an SS officer came, and he starts counting. He goes 5, 10, 15-- we were five abreast. He counted everybody. We were allowed to go back into the barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We are being given a piece of bread. The-- a loaf of bread was as big, approximately, as a brick. And that loaf of bread was for three people. So each three people would have to-- we would cut that loaf of bread as accurate as possible, so nobody got a bigger piece than the next guy or a smaller piece than the next guy. And that was our food for the day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're-- we're let out of the barracks. And we're walking around. And we're seeing other people that came from other ghettos that were in the same clothing that we were in, in these striped prisoner clothes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- some of us still don't believe, or don't want to believe after talking to the older prisoners that were there, that our mothers and our rest of our families are not alive anymore. It's only the following day. Some of us [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] believe what happened, and some of us don't want to believe what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you believe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I didn't think it happened. I didn't think it happened. We ran into my father's younger brother, who came from a different city, in a different ghetto that we hadn't seen before for a few years. We ran into him. And we made up to meet him the following day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we never saw him again. His name is Abe Stern, who lives in Montreal today. We never saw him after that short meeting. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A day or two later after we're in Auschwitz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- they tell all kids 14 years and younger to sleep in the-- in the same barrack, but in a different part of the barrack. We didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't believe what happened to our parents and to-- and to our mothers. Instead of us hiding and trying to pass off for older, we just listened. And we just did it, not knowing why they-- why they're asking us to do that. In fact, some of the people, the older people, tell us, you're going to be taken to your mothers. You will listen to your mother. Make-- make sure that you don't give her a hard time. Tell your mother that we're OK. They thought that they-- none of us knew. As luck would have it, a day or two later, this law was rescinded. Because they made up a transport of people, and they needed so many bodies to send to some other place. We didn't know where, but we're told that we will be going to another camp. Every morning when we get up those few days that we were there, every morning when we get up for [NON-ENGLISH], that counting and standing in line is called [NON-ENGLISH]. When we get out, it was like 4 or 5 AM. It was still dark. The stars were still out. We saw people on the-- we were surrounded with electric wire. Some people that couldn't take it just, during the night, walked out, and they grabbed the wire. In the morning, we used to see them in different positions where they committed suicide. They didn't-- they-- they just-- they couldn't take it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We weren't in Auschwitz too long, which was lucky for me. Because, otherwise, I probably wouldn't make it, 'cause that was a bad place. They moved us to a railroad again. We come into the railroad station and same cars as the cars-- same type of cars as the cars that brought us there. Only, now, we're all in prisoner clothes. And everybody has to sit down, open your legs, and another man sits in front of you. The other man opens his legs like that and another man sits in front of you. And we're all loaded tight. In the middle, there is all the room that you have for the guards. The guards are [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yugoslavian-speaking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Croatian SS. They spoke Yugoslavian. They probably couldn't even speak German well. But they were SS. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Aside from their weapons, they had a long stick that would reach to any end of the wagon. They tell us only one person can get up at a time. If more than one person gets up, you get shot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're in the train. We're going for several days. I'm not sure of the date. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We arrive in a place we never heard of. The place is called Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We get off the train in Buchenwald. We're lined up. We're-- they always marched us like soldiers would march. And they would always say, left, right, left, 1, 2, 3. And we would have to walk that way. They walked-- they walked us into the camp. And we're assigned to-- to barracks again, what they call blocks. And each block had what they called the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the guy who was in charge of that particular barrack. And from there on, from there on on, nobody is-- nobody is called by name anymore at this point. Everybody is called by their number. They called us [NON-ENGLISH], which means prisoners. And if they called somebody, they would say, [NON-ENGLISH] number so and so. And if you were that number, you knew they were calling you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, we're in Buchenwald. Same things with the [NON-ENGLISH] every morning. We're standing there, whether you're sick, whether you drop. You have to stand on that [NON-ENGLISH] in the morning. Same kind of food, they give us a piece of bread. The end of the day, you get a watered-down type of soup called kohlrabi soup. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We're not working at this point. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us all in-- they took pictures of each one of us from the chest up, like a prisoner would have, like you see here in-- like you see here in the post office, for example. Why, I don't know. We never saw those pictures. But they took pictures of us. And we're just-- they're just keeping us. We're warehoused, so to speak, over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, I'm still with my father and with the same group. We're keeping-- we're all keeping together, the same group that we started out from our town, that we started out from the ghetto, some of the people that we made acquaintances in the ghetto. We're still the same group there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up to this point, who-- who were you relying on in Auschwitz and Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nobody I can rely on. Because even though I was with my father, which was nice, but there was nobody to rely on, because everybody was in the same boat. Nobody could help anybody. Nobody can be of any assistance to anybody. But it was nice that I was with my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father's brother-in-law, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] his name-- we-- we called him [INAUDIBLE]. He wasn't a grandfather, but that was his nickname. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had a brother and a sister that lived in Belgium. And when we were in Buchenwald, he got busy. We met people from different parts of the world there. There were prisoners there from different parts, from different countries. Wherever Germany came in, the Jews were deported. So he was busy looking for his brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He could-- he did not find him, but he got busy looking for him. After being in Buchenwald for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what would be maybe a couple of weeks, they started to call names and numbers. And they put-- they would be put on-- on trucks. And people-- every day, a group of people would leave. We didn't know where they-- where they went. But we sort of-- after two or three groups leaving, we sort of were hoping and thought that the group that is going today is going to meet the group that left yesterday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was my turn. And my number and name-- my number comes up. I'm on the truck going someplace. My father is not with me. My father says goodbye to me, but in a very light tone. Because he figured tomorrow he'll be on the transport, and we'll meet in the next place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's when I saw my father the last time until seven years later. He stayed in Buchenwald and survived Buchenwald. And I am going to a camp. When I get there, I see the camp is called Dora. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you were in this transport from Buchenwald to Dora?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seems like a couple of hundred each day. I'm not sure, but it would seem to me there were a couple of hundred each day that left Buchenwald to go to Dora.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left, your group, how many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. Same as all the rest of the groups that would leave days before us. But I have no idea. I would imagine maybe 200 people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We arrive in a camp called Dora. When we get to that camp, that was a working camp. There were prisoners there before us. And mind you, there-- and that camp had prisoners, not only Jews. There were came-- there were prisoners with the same striped clothes that we were that were there for political reasons. There were homosexuals. They were there, because they committed murder. There were-- there were prisoners there for reasons that people go to a jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what people were, political--

speaker: interviewee
text: We all-- we all recognized each other by-- each one of us had a number. And next to the number, you had a-- a color triangle sort of. And the triangle would-- the Jews, Jewish people had a yellow. Other-- the political prisoners had a red. Purple was a homosexual. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Green was a murderer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the first time that you had heard what homosexual was? Did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what homosexual was before. I know now so I-- to talk about it. But at that point, I didn't know. We saw the-- we learned to know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what all these numbers-- all these colors next to their numbers meant. In that color was also the nationality-- R for Russia, P for Poland, F for France. So we knew that this prisoner is a political prisoner from France or from Belgium or Holland. We had people from everywhere that Germany occupied. We met people in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a working camp. When we got there, the prisoners in that camp, being there were older prisoners there, and they saw the yellow colors next to our numbers, they knew we were Jewish. And they said, there are still Jews alive. Can you imagine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, they are assigning us to work-- to work details. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were-- Dora had a big tunnel. In that tunnel, they produced the V-1 and V-2 rockets, those pilotless rockets that they shot into England, which we didn't know what V-1 and V-2 meant at that time. I, certainly, as a young kid at that time, didn't know. But there were some of the political prisoners, and there were some prisoners of war people that were there. They knew what it was. But we didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There, we're being assigned to different work details. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm assigned to a detail where we had to go to the railroad station. And they had-- that camp at the time was being expanded. And they had these prefabricated barracks on the railroad cars. We had to go. And there were big, like, walls, pieces of walls. And we brought them into the camp. There were other people that were assembling them, and made-- made them into a barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those walls, they assigned five or six people to carry. Those walls had to be carried on shoulders. So people would be supporting those walls on their shoulders.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was our first taste of work in the camps. We had to run. We couldn't walk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] People were asking for water. Because it was, at that point, I would say, it was in the end of May, beginning of June. It was hot already. They wouldn't let us drink water. And we had to run. I was a little boy of 14. I wasn't tall enough for that thing to rest on my-- on my shoulder. But I just had to be part of this group that are carrying that piece of barrack. And we had to run. And every time we-- we used to go out of the camp-- was a-- was a gate going out of the camp. Whenever we went out of the camp, they would say, 50 people going out of the camp. And whenever we came back into the camp, they would have to say the same amount of people entering the camp. Because they kept an eye on people shouldn't escape. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the top of the camp it had-- it had a wording in German, [NON-ENGLISH], work, makes free. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As we-- the day went on, and we-- we-- we were made to run with those barracks. Every time we used to come back into the camp, and if we went out with 50, the guy used to say, five dead. So nobody asked any questions. 45 walked in. 5 just died, and nobody-- there was-- nobody asked for any accounting as to how and why.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
